critic_raw:
Accuracy is the primary metric for a multi-class cancer type and stage classification task on histopathology images. The design acknowledges class imbalance exists (secondary metrics include per-class sensitivity/specificity 'to identify if any cancer type is systematically under- or over-detected') and uses McNemar's test on accuracy for the comparison. However, in a multi-class classification with skewed class frequencies, overall accuracy is dominated by performance on the most prevalent classes. A model that perfectly classifies the common cancer types while failing entirely on rare stages would achieve high accuracy. The design's own acknowledgment that imbalance requires per-class metrics contradicts the choice of accuracy as primary metric.

The baseline logistic regression uses 'default hyperparameters, specifically C=1.0' explicitly without tuning, while the CNN receives full hyperparameter tuning and early stopping on validation accuracy. The stated rationale ('provides a practical baseline without the confound of manual hyperparameter tuning') is incorrect — untuned logistic regression is not a 'control' for tuning confounds; it is a weaker logistic regression. The comparison is tuned CNN vs. untuned logistic regression, not CNN vs. logistic regression. For a methodologically valid comparison, the logistic regression should receive equivalent tuning effort (grid search over C on the validation set). The design explicitly acknowledges 'equivalent preprocessing and tuning effort' as necessary for fair comparison but then applies unequal tuning.

The CNN uses ResNet-50 pre-trained on ImageNet while the baseline uses hand-crafted features (color histograms, LBP, Haralick textures, morphological features). These differ in both feature learning approach and feature type. A fair architecture comparison would require either (a) CNN without ImageNet pretraining vs. hand-crafted features, isolating representation learning, or (b) a logistic regression trained on CNN-extracted features vs. CNN fine-tuned end-to-end, isolating the classifier. As designed, the comparison tests 'ImageNet-pretrained deep learning' vs. 'hand-crafted classical features' — a legitimate applied question, but not an architecture comparison.

The inter-rater agreement is computed on a stratified 10% subset (1,000 images) and images with disagreement are excluded. If the 10% audit is not representative of the full 10,000 images, some disagreement-prone image types may not be identified and excluded. The 90% of images not audited may contain label noise that inflates both models' apparent performance through incorrect ground truth rather than distinguishing their relative capabilities.
all_issues_raised: Accuracy is the primary metric for an imbalanced multi-class histopathology task — a model that fails entirely on rare cancer stages can achieve high accuracy through perfect performance on common stages, and the design's own secondary metrics acknowledge this inadequacy.

The logistic regression baseline uses default C=1.0 without tuning while the CNN receives full hyperparameter tuning and early stopping, explicitly violating the design's own stated requirement of 'equivalent tuning effort' and measuring tuned CNN vs. untuned logistic regression.

The CNN uses ImageNet pretraining while the baseline uses hand-crafted features — the comparison conflates representation learning method (pretrained deep features vs. hand-crafted features) with classifier architecture, making the source of any performance difference unattributable.

Inter-rater agreement is computed on a 10% audit subset; the remaining 90% of images may contain label noise from unidentified disagreements, inflating both models' apparent performance without affecting their relative ranking in a principled way.
all_issues_adjudicated: Accuracy is the primary metric for an imbalanced multi-class histopathology task — a model that fails entirely on rare cancer stages can achieve high accuracy through perfect performance on common stages, and the design's own secondary metrics acknowledge this inadequacy.

The logistic regression baseline uses default C=1.0 without tuning while the CNN receives full hyperparameter tuning and early stopping, explicitly violating the design's own stated requirement of 'equivalent tuning effort' and measuring tuned CNN vs. untuned logistic regression.

The CNN uses ImageNet pretraining while the baseline uses hand-crafted features — the comparison conflates representation learning method (pretrained deep features vs. hand-crafted features) with classifier architecture, making the source of any performance difference unattributable.

Inter-rater agreement is computed on a 10% audit subset; the remaining 90% of images may contain label noise from unidentified disagreements, inflating both models' apparent performance without affecting their relative ranking in a principled way.
